articles.title: Severe heatwave in India: Delhi reaches 44.5°C on April 25 - The News Mill
articles.full_text:
India is enduring a severe heatwave as temperatures continue to rise across the country, severely disrupting daily life. In Delhi, the temperature reached 44.5 degrees Celsius on April 25, according to the India Meteorological Department (IMD). The heatwave has resulted in reduced public movement, especially during peak afternoon hours, with residents opting to stay indoors to avoid exposure to the sun.
People venturing outside for work or essential activities are taking precautions such as using umbrellas, wearing protective clothing, and consuming cold beverages and seasonal fruits to combat dehydration and fatigue caused by the extreme heat. The IMD has forecast that the intense heat conditions will persist over the next few days, with maximum temperatures in Delhi expected to range between 42 and 44 degrees Celsius, approximately 3.0 to 5.1 degrees Celsius above the seasonal average.
Surface winds from the northwest are anticipated to reach speeds up to 20 kmph in the afternoon, decreasing to below 15 kmph during the evening and night while continuing from the northwest.
Other regions, including Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Rajasthan, and Odisha, have also experienced soaring temperatures. Prayagraj in Uttar Pradesh recorded the highest temperature at 45.2 degrees Celsius. Several districts in Bihar and Odisha remain under intense heatwave conditions, with the IMD issuing alerts due to significantly above-normal temperatures.
Bihar districts including Kaimur, Rohtas, Aurangabad, Buxar, Bhojpur (Arrah), and Arwal are currently affected by the heatwave. IMD Scientist Ashish Kumar stated, “Temperatures remain above normal today, but conditions are expected to ease from tomorrow. Heatwave risks will reduce, and temperatures are likely to return to normal with the possibility of rainfall.”
In Odisha, extreme temperatures have been recorded, with Jharsuguda reaching 44.8 degrees Celsius and Titlagarh in Balangir district recording 44 degrees Celsius. Eighteen stations have recorded temperatures above 40 degrees Celsius. IMD Scientist Rajasree VPM said, “After three days, temperatures are expected to drop by 2-4 degrees Celsius over the following two to three days. The government and IMD are advising people to avoid peak afternoon hours and stay hydrated.” She added, “From day four onwards, isolated thunderstorms with gusty winds of 40-50 kmph are likely, which may bring relief from the ongoing heatwave conditions.”
Central Karnataka’s Chitradurga region, noted for its historic forts, is also experiencing intense heat. Residents and faculty members of Government Science College report increased cases of dehydration and fatigue amid limited rainfall relief. Associate Professor Sheema Kausher commented, “People are afraid to step out during peak hours due to the scorching heat radiating from the fort rocks. Compared to the last two years, the intensity of the sun has increased significantly, and this is affecting health.” Associate Professor Ramesh Bhat added, “Earlier, rainfall used to bring down the temperature. But now, despite irregular rain, the heat has increased. People are falling sick due to the harsh sun,” expressing concerns over worsening conditions.
Madhya Pradesh’s Bhopal has recorded temperatures exceeding 42 degrees Celsius, accompanied by intense hot winds known as “loo”. Residents have turned to traditional cooling drinks such as lemon water (shikanji), sugarcane juice, and raw mango drink (aam panna) to cope. Street vendors report heavy footfall with continuous queues.
In Rajasthan’s Bikaner district, summer heat arrived early, with temperatures already surpassing 43 degrees Celsius in April. The prevailing heatwave has significantly affected normal life, leaving streets mostly deserted as residents remain indoors to avoid the intense heat and strong winds. Officials have advised the public to take necessary precautions amid the prevailing hot weather conditions.
